planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; Ifafa lily is one of the most shade-tolerant Cyrtanthus species, growing naturally in the forest margins and coastal bush of KwaZulu-Natal near the Ifafa River. It blooms in summer, producing nodding, tubular, white to cream flowers with a light, sweet fragrance. All parts are toxic if ingested. More compact and suitable for containers than the larger Cyrtanthus elatus.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; Ifafa lily is a small, charming, shade-loving South African Cyrtanthus native to the forest margins and coastal bush of the Ifafa River area in KwaZulu-Natal — one of the most shade-tolerant members of a genus that is generally sun-preferring. In summer it produces from each small, narrow-bulbed plant a neat umbel of 6–10 nodding, slender, tubular, pure-white to creamy-white flowers on a thin stem — the individual tubes slightly curved and pendant, slightly fragrant, clustering in a small, graceful, draping umbel. The form is more delicate and smaller than the large, bold C. elatus (Scarborough Lily), and the nodding, white, tubular flowers create a quiet, refined charm suited to woodland and shaded container gardens. The narrow, strap-shaped, bright-green leaves are attractive year-round in mild climates. For zone 9 shade gardens where a small, neat, summer-flowering white lily with genuine shade tolerance is needed, it provides something few bulbs can offer — a compact, shade-happy summer white. Outstanding as a container plant for a shaded terrace or woodland garden.

subcategory: tall blue-flowered South African iris relative

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; blue sceptre is a larger, more robust species than the commonly grown A. ecklonii — forming substantial, bold clumps of iris-like sword-leaves and producing in late spring and summer tall, branching spikes of small but intensely blue flowers. The individual flowers last only one day but are produced in rapid succession from clustered, wiry buds. It self-sows freely in mild-climate gardens in suitable conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; blue sceptre is the largest and most impressive Aristea species — a South African iris-family perennial of the Western Cape fynbos that forms substantial architectural clumps of erect, sword-shaped, bright-green iris leaves 3–4 feet tall and in late spring through summer produces tall, branching, candelabra-like flowering scapes carrying dense clusters of small, vivid, saturated, sky-blue to gentian-blue, six-petaled flowers that are individually ephemeral (lasting one day) but collectively produced in rapid succession so that a plant in full bloom carries numerous bright-blue flowers simultaneously. The clear, pure, deep blue of the flowers — a typical aristea characteristic — is a distinctive and beautiful color in the summer perennial border, and at the scale of A. capitata with its tall scapes, the blue is deployed in an architectural structure of considerable presence. It thrives in the semi-shade of woodland margins and dry shade of large shrubs in zone 9 Gulf Coast conditions. Once established it self-sows modestly in suitable moist-to-dry shade garden soils.

planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; New Year lily is a Cape winter-rainfall species that blooms in winter (January in South Africa, its summer; December–February in zone 9 cultivation), producing vivid scarlet-red flowers with white teardrop markings on the lower petals. It is native to wet mountain seeps and waterfall margins (hence 'Waterfall Gladiolus') in the Western Cape. This species is one of the parent plants of the modern garden gladiolus hybrid complex.

directions: Plant corms in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; New Year lily has a historical distinction that makes it botanically significant beyond its beauty: it is one of the primary wild species from which the entire complex of modern hybrid garden gladioli was developed, contributing especially the brilliant scarlet-red color and the broad, well-formed floret shape that characterize the best hybrid gladioli. In its own right it is a beautiful species — producing in winter (unusually early, hence 'New Year lily') tall spikes of vivid, clear, true-scarlet to cardinal-red, hooded, gladiolus-type flowers with two distinctive, teardrop-shaped, white blotches on the lower three petals, the white markings providing a crisp, contrasting accent to the vivid scarlet. It grows naturally on wet mountain cliffs and waterfall margins in the Cederberg and Hottentots Holland Mountains of the Western Cape — habitats of constant water seepage and mist — and therefore tolerates and prefers the moist conditions of its native waterfall-spray habitat better than most gladioli. The winter bloom timing, in December through February, creates the unusual situation of brilliant-red gladiolus flowers in the zone 9 midwinter garden.

subcategory: spectacular large South African autumn bulb

planting.note: Plant large bulbs with the upper half exposed above the soil surface in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Josephine's lily flowers in late summer and autumn (March–April in South Africa; August–October in zone 9), producing flowers before the leaves emerge (hysteranthous). The spectacular candelabra flower head develops from a leafless scape. This is a very large bulb requiring patience — 4–7 years from seed to first flower. It is named for Empress Josephine of France, who grew it at Malmaison.

directions: Plant large bulbs (upper half exposed) in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Josephine's lily is one of the most spectacular of all South African bulbs — producing in late summer and early autumn a floral display of almost theatrical grandeur: a fat, stout, leafless scape emerging from the large, round-shouldered, papery-tunic bulb and rising to 24–36 inches, then expanding at the apex into an enormous, spherical, candelabra-like umbel of 20–40 or more individual flowers, each on its own radiating, arching pedicel, the whole structure spanning 24 inches or more across — a complete ball of brilliant, deep-pink to carmine-pink to salmon-red, narrowly tubular-funnel flowers with reflexing petal tips and projecting stamens, creating an unmistakable silhouette of a giant, pink fireworks burst frozen in botanical form. After the spectacular flower head, the strap-like leaves emerge and persist through winter and spring. The bulb is named for Empress Josephine Bonaparte, who cultivated it at Château de Malmaison from bulbs obtained from South Africa. A plant of exceptional scale, patience (4–7 years from seed or purchased offset to first flower), and spectacular reward for the zone 9 garden's most dramatic space.

subcategory: original yellow-orange South African crocosmia species

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; golden falling stars is the parent species of the widely grown hybrid C. x crocosmiiflora (Montbretia) and one of two parents of modern large-flowered crocosmia hybrids. It blooms in late summer (August–September), slightly later than the hybrid Montbretia. The flowers are noticeably larger than those of C. x crocosmiiflora and an attractive golden-yellow to golden-orange. It spreads vigorously by corm chains.

directions: Plant corms in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; golden falling stars is one of the two wild South African Crocosmia species — along with C. pottsii — from which the immensely popular hybrid Montbretia (C. x crocosmiiflora) was developed in 1880 by the French nurseryman Victor Lemoine by crossing C. aurea with C. pottsii. In its own right, C. aurea is a very attractive plant: somewhat taller and more imposing than the hybrid montbretia, with larger, more open, golden-yellow to warm golden-orange funnel-shaped flowers on arching, branching stems in late summer and early autumn. The golden-yellow color is more purely yellow and less orange than many crocosmia cultivars, providing a clean, warm-gold effect in the late-summer border. Like all crocosmias it spreads vigorously by producing chains of linked corms underground, forming ever-expanding colonies of the arching sword-like leaves and arching, golden-flowered stems. It tolerates more shade than full-sun crocosmia recommendations suggest, doing well in the dappled shade of deciduous trees or in part-shade border situations. The parent species of the most-planted corm in temperate gardens.
